beats.say: That $R \approx 40.8$ m is a real answer — but is it the *best* answer? There's a beautiful general formula hiding in the same two-step approach we just used. For any launch angle $\theta$, the range simplifies to $R = \dfrac{v_0^2 \sin(2\theta)}{g}$.

The key is that $\sin(2\theta)$ sitting in the numerator. Sine is at most 1 — and it hits exactly 1 when $2\theta = 90°$, meaning $\theta = 45°$. Every other angle gives a smaller sine, and therefore a shorter range.
Let's make all the angles compete. Here come five trajectories at once — $15°$, $30°$, $45°$, $60°$, and $75°$ — all launched at the same $20$ m/s. Watch how high and how far each one goes.

Now look at where they land. The gold arc — $45°$ — reaches the farthest right. And notice something elegant: $30°$ and $60°$ share the same landing spot, and so do $15°$ and $75°$. Symmetric pairs, perfectly balanced around $45°$.

card.content: Complementary angles share the same range: $R(\theta) = R(90°{-}\theta)$, because $\sin(2\theta) = \sin(180°{-}2\theta)$.

beats.say: Here's the rigorous picture. A graph of $f(\theta) = \sin(2\theta)$ slides up beneath the trajectories. It rises from zero, crests at $\theta = 45°$, and falls back to zero at $90°$. The peak is unmistakable — and that's exactly where our ball was aimed.
The golden dot on the graph connects back up to the tallest arc in the fan — our $45°$ trajectory. This isn't a coincidence or a lucky number. It's the geometry of sine forcing the answer.

Plug in: $R = \dfrac{(20)^2 \cdot \sin(90°)}{9.8} = \dfrac{400}{9.8} \approx 40.8$ meters. That's not just *an* answer — it's the *maximum* possible range at this speed. No other angle gets you farther. $45°$ is the champion.

card.title: Why 45° Wins

content.value: Bonus: complementary angles give equal ranges, e.g. $R(30°) = R(60°)$.
